title: Somatic Experiencing for Energy Surges
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at the ceiling, feeling like your insides are vibrating, or maybe you're just wired beyond belief, body buz
body:
Somatic Experiencing for Energy Surges

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at the ceiling, feeling like your insides are vibrating, or maybe you're just wired beyond belief, body buzzing with something you can't name. You've Googled "spiritual emergency," "kundalini crisis," "why does my body feel like it's going to explode?" Right? I know that feeling. I've been there. That raw, terrifying aloneness when your body becomes a foreign, unpredictable entity. It's not fun. It's not peaceful. It's a goddamn nightmare sometimes. And people tell you it's a blessing. Bullshit. Not when you're in the thick of it, feeling like you're losing your mind, your body, your very self.

You've probably tried everything. Meditating more, meditating less. Grounding exercises that just make you feel more ungrounded. Maybe you've even seen doctors who look at you like you're crazy, or worse, tell you it's anxiety and offer you pills that don't touch the real problem. I've been through it all. The sleepless nights, the involuntary movements, the heat, the cold, the sheer, overwhelming energy coursing through me. It felt like I was being electrocuted from the inside out, sometimes. And the panic that comes with that? It's indescribable. But here's the thing: you're not crazy. You're not alone. And there are ways to work with this. One of the most powerful tools I found, one that actually helped me get my feet back on the ground, was Somatic Experiencing (SE).

Look, I'm not going to sugarcoat it. This journey is messy. It's scary. But it's also real, whatever you want to call it - kundalini awakening, spiritual emergence, spiritual emergency. It's your nervous system going through something deep, and it needs help. Not just talk therapy that only lives in your head, but something that speaks directly to the body, because that's where this energy is living, right? That's where it's screaming. That's where it's stuck. And that's exactly what SE does.

What Even Is This "Energy Surge" You're Talking About?

So, you're probably feeling this intense energy. It's not just nerves, you know? It's different. It can manifest as:

  • Intense heat or cold sensations
  • Involuntary movements - tremors, shaking, jerking, spasms
  • A feeling of electricity or buzzing inside your body
  • Overwhelming pressure, especially in the head or spine
  • Difficulty sleeping, or needing very little sleep
  • Sudden bursts of energy followed by crashes
  • Intense emotional swings - bliss, terror, rage, grief, all in a short span
  • Vivid dreams or nightmares
  • Feeling disconnected from your body, or hyper-aware of every sensation
  • A sense of internal pressure, like something wants to break out

Does that sound familiar? I bet it does. For me, it was a constant, internal vibration, like a low-frequency hum that never stopped. My body would shake uncontrollably, especially at night. I'd get these sudden rushes of heat that would make me feel like I was burning up from the inside. And the anxiety? Oh god, the anxiety. It was a constant companion. It felt like my nervous system was stuck in overdrive, unable to turn off. It was like living in a constant state of fight or flight, but without any external threat. It was my own body that was the threat, or at least, that's how it felt.

The Kundalini Connection

Many people experiencing these intense energy surges identify it as a kundalini awakening or spiritual emergence. I've read a ton of books on it - The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is a classic that really helped me feel less alone, and The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) gave me some medical perspective, which was strangely reassuring. These experiences aren't new. People have been going through this for centuries, across cultures. It's often described as a powerful, dormant energy at the base of the spine that awakens and moves upwards, purifying and changing everything in its path. Sounds poetic, right? But when it's happening to you, it feels anything but poetic. It feels like you're being torn apart. And sometimes, that energy gets stuck. Or it overwhelms your system. That's where the crisis comes in. That's where the spiritual emergency happens. Your nervous system just can't handle the wattage.

It's like plugging a massive industrial machine into a household outlet. The wiring just isn't built for it. And what happens? Fuses blow. Sparks fly. Everything goes haywire. That's what's happening to you. Your nervous system, which has been conditioned by trauma, stress, modern life - it's not ready for this immense energetic download. It freaks out. It goes into survival mode. And that's where Somatic Experiencing comes in, because it works directly with that survival response in your body.

Trauma and Energy - They're Connected

Here's the truth is: a lot of these energy surges, especially the ones that feel overwhelming and scary, are connected to unresolved trauma. Not necessarily "big T" trauma, though it absolutely can be. It can be "little t" traumas - chronic stress, neglect, emotional wounds, early attachment issues. Your body remembers everything. It stores all that unresolved energy, all that fight, flight, freeze, or fawn response that never got completed. And when this new, powerful energy starts moving, it can stir up all that old, stuck stuff. It's like a pressure cooker. The new energy turns up the heat, and all the old, suppressed emotions and sensations start bubbling to the surface. It can feel like you're re-experiencing old traumas, even if you don't consciously remember them. You're just feeling the raw, unfiltered sensations in your body. It's terrifying, I know.

Somatic Experiencing: The Game Changer

So, you're probably wondering, "Okay, Kalesh, what the hell is SE and how is it going to help me stop feeling like a human tuning fork?" Good question. Somatic Experiencing, developed by Peter Levine, is a body-oriented approach to healing trauma and other stress-related issues. It's based on the idea that trauma isn't just a psychological wound; it's a physiological one. It's energy that gets trapped in the body when an animal (that's you!) can't complete its natural fight, flight, or freeze response to a perceived threat. Think about a gazelle being chased by a lion. If it escapes, it shakes, trembles, discharges all that excess energy, and then goes back to grazing. Humans, with our big brains, often override that natural discharge. We "tough it out." We suppress. We intellectualize. And that energy gets stuck. When an energy surge happens, it can kick all that stuck energy into overdrive.

SE doesn't focus on talking about the story of your trauma as much as it focuses on the bodily sensations associated with it. Because it's those sensations that are holding the charge. It's those sensations that are making you feel like you're going to explode. It's a gentle, slow, and respectful process. It's about helping your nervous system complete what it couldn't complete before, allowing that trapped energy to discharge safely and gradually. It's about bringing your body back into regulation, back into a state where it feels safe and calm again. Know what I mean?

How SE Works with Energy Surges

Here's how SE can help with those overwhelming energy surges:

  1. Titration: This is a big one. You don't dump all the trauma or all the energy on yourself at once. That's what created the crisis in the first place. Instead, an SE practitioner helps you work with very small, manageable "doses" of sensation. You dip a toe in the water, not jump into the deep end. You might feel a tiny bit of the buzzing, a fleeting moment of heat, and then come back to a place of safety. This teaches your nervous system that it can handle these sensations without being overwhelmed. It's about building capacity.
  2. Pendulation: This involves gently moving your attention between uncomfortable sensations (like the buzzing or heat) and comfortable or neutral sensations (like the feeling of your feet on the floor, or a calm spot in your body). This back-and-forth movement helps your nervous system learn to self-regulate. It shows your body that it can move out of distress and back into a state of calm. It's like a nervous system exercise, building its strength and resilience.
  3. Tracking Sensation: Instead of focusing on the story of "why" you're feeling this, SE helps you focus purely on the raw, physical sensations in your body. Where exactly is the heat? What's its texture? Does it move? Is it vibrating? This might sound counterintuitive when you're terrified of the sensations, but it actually helps you create a conscious relationship with them, rather than just being overwhelmed by them. It's like observing a wild animal from a safe distance, instead of being attacked by it.
  4. Discharge: As you work through the sensations, your body might naturally start to discharge the trapped energy. This can look like trembling, shaking, yawning, sighing, crying, or even stomach gurgles. This is a good thing! It's your body's natural way of releasing stress and returning to equilibrium. It's not a sign you're losing control; it's a sign you're regaining it.
  5. Resourcing: This is about building internal and external resources that help you feel safe and grounded. It could be a calm memory, a supportive person, a place in nature, or even a specific sensation in your body that feels pleasant or neutral. When the energy gets too intense, you can turn to these resources to help regulate your system.

I'll tell you what, when I first started SE, I was skeptical. I'd tried everything. But the gentle, slow pace, the focus on my body, not just my thoughts - it was different. My practitioner would have me notice the subtle trembling in my hands, or the warmth spreading in my chest, and then gently guide me to notice my feet on the floor, or the support of the chair. And slowly, agonizingly slowly at first, I started to feel a shift. The constant internal hum would lessen, even if just for a few minutes. I'd get moments of peace. And those moments grew longer. It gave me hope when I had none. It helped me understand that these sensations weren't going to kill me, even if they felt like they would. They were just energy, stuck energy, trying to move. And SE gave me the tools to help it move safely.

Finding Support and Moving Forward

You can't do this alone. Trust me, I tried. It's too much. You need someone who understands, someone who won't dismiss you, someone who can hold space for the intensity of what you're going through. Here are some things to consider:

  • Find a Somatic Experiencing Practitioner: Look for someone certified in SE. You can find directories online. Interview a few. Find someone you feel safe with. That feeling of safety is everything when your nervous system is in overdrive.
  • Grounding Practices: Even if they feel useless sometimes, keep trying. Simple things like feeling your feet on the ground, noticing five things you can see, hear, touch. Deep belly breathing. Spending time in nature. Heavy blankets. Warm baths. Whatever brings you even a tiny bit of comfort and presence.
  • Lifestyle Adjustments: This isn't a time for pushing yourself. Rest. Eat nourishing food. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar. Limit screen time, especially before bed. Prioritize sleep, even if it's elusive. Your body is doing intense work, and it needs all the support it can get.
  • Educate Yourself (Carefully): Reading about these experiences can be validating. But be careful not to get caught in a loop of fear or obsession. I found When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) to be really helpful and compassionate resources. They offer a broader context without being overly alarmist or dismissive.
  • Build a Support System: If you have trusted friends or family, talk to them. If they don't understand, that's okay. Find online communities or support groups for spiritual emergence. Knowing you're not the only one going through this can be a lifeline.

So yeah, you're in a tough spot. It feels like the world is ending, or at least your world. But it's not. This is a process, a sometimes brutal one, but a process nonetheless. Your body is trying to heal, to integrate, to expand. It's just doing it in a way that feels incredibly scary right now. Somatic Experiencing won't make the energy go away overnight, but it will give you the tools to work with it, to regulate your nervous system, and to slowly, gently, bring yourself back to a place of safety and peace. It's about learning to ride the waves instead of being drowned by them. You've got this. You really do. Just take it one breath, one sensation, one tiny step at a time. I promise you, there's a way through this. I found it, and you can too.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
